policy_statement: Electrify 30% of total existing residential buildings by 2035.
mechanism_description: Electrifying existing homes replaces on-site fossil fuel equipment with electric alternatives, reducing direct building fossil fuel combustion and associated greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy technology switch (fossil → electric) → ↓ on-site fossil combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Retrofit/replace residential fossil fuel end-use equipment (e.g., gas/oil furnaces/boilers, gas water heaters, gas stoves) with electric alternatives (e.g., heat pumps, electric resistance/HP water heaters, induction/electric cooking).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of methane/oil in buildings lowers direct CO₂ emissions (and reduces methane leakage risk from downstream distribution by lowering throughput); emissions may shift to centralized electricity generation depending on grid mix.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions from residential energy use (immediate reduction in Scope 1; overall mitigation strengthened as the electricity grid decarbonizes).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from avoided on-site residential fossil fuel combustion (and associated upstream fuel supply emissions) via electrification.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Technology fuel-switch in residences reduces on-site fossil fuel combustion, yielding lower GHG emissions—this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can improve energy efficiency (Resource Efficiency) and resilience/health, the defining and largest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction from eliminating direct fossil combustion.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is very carbon-intensive, near-term net emissions reductions could be smaller or delayed; however, the mechanism still targets emissions mitigation via electrification and typically assumes/aligns with grid decarbonization trajectories.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that electrifies existing residential buildings with the intent/structure described necessarily reduces direct fossil fuel use; thus Mitigation remains the primary category for the entire mechanism class.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy also primarily functions as efficiency, equity, or resilience intervention depends on policy-specific design (e.g., complementary weatherization, backup power, demand flexibility) and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In jurisdictions with highly fossil-heavy electricity and no decarbonization plan, electrification may shift emissions from buildings to the power sector in the short term; nevertheless, the mechanism’s dominant intended pathway is direct fossil fuel combustion reduction in buildings (mitigation). Additional coupled measures (weatherization, demand response) can change magnitude but not the primary category.
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved indoor air quality (reduced combustion pollutants), reduced exposure during extreme heat via efficient heat pumps (cooling), potential resilience benefits if paired with efficiency/storage; not explicitly stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: No implementation mechanism is specified (e.g., rebates, mandates, performance standards), so classification is based solely on the presence of a quantitative time-bound target.
